title: Redundant index on room_actions (room_id, seq) duplicates the unique constraint index
evidence: Line 42: `CONSTRAINT room_actions_room_seq_unique UNIQUE (room_id, seq)` implicitly creates a btree index. Line 46: `CREATE INDEX room_actions_room_id_seq_idx ON room_actions (room_id, seq ASC)` creates a second identical index on the same columns in the same order. Postgres maintains both, doubling write overhead for every INSERT.
impact: Every room_actions INSERT pays the cost of maintaining two identical indexes. No correctness risk; pure wasted I/O and storage.
recommendation: DROP the explicit index (`room_actions_room_id_seq_idx`) — the unique constraint index already covers the replay read path (`ORDER BY seq ASC` on `room_id`).
acceptance_criteria: The explicit index is removed in a new migration
The replay SELECT + submit_room_action INSERT still perform correctly under EXPLAIN ANALYZE

title: rate_limits table grows unbounded for distinct attacker IPs that are never seen again
evidence: bump_rate_limit() deletes stale windows only for the SAME bucket (line 52: `WHERE bucket = p_bucket AND window_start < p_window - 1`). If an attacker IP hits create_room 10 times in one window and is never seen again, its bucket row persists forever — the cleanup only fires when that exact bucket is incremented again. With N distinct attacker IPs, the table accumulates N rows with no expiry path. There is no scheduled job or TTL column.
impact: Under a burst of distinct-IP abuse the table grows without bound. At large scale this degrades INSERT performance (index bloat) and consumes storage. At small scale (a hobbyist project) the practical risk is low but the table will never self-clean for dead buckets.
recommendation: Add a periodic cleanup job (pg_cron or an Edge Function cron) that runs `DELETE FROM rate_limits WHERE window_start < floor(extract(epoch from now())/60) - 2` to remove rows older than 2 minutes. Alternatively add a `created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()` column and apply a Postgres TTL policy (or pg_partman) for automatic expiry.
acceptance_criteria: A scheduled or lazy cleanup path removes rows for buckets not seen in >2 windows
The table does not grow indefinitely under a scan of distinct IPs

title: Schema↔code drift: client SupabaseTypes.ts types RoomAction.action as NetworkFireAction only, but the log now stores 4 action types
evidence: SupabaseTypes.ts line 30: `action: NetworkFireAction` — typed as fire-only. The schema comment on line 38 of 001_init.sql says `{ type: 'fire', angle: number, power: number, weapon: string }` (also fire-only), but submit_action/index.ts lines 43-67 defines and commits 4 types: `fire`, `use_shield`, `buy`, `next_round`. The TS types in SupabaseTypes.ts have not been updated to reflect the expanded action union. Any code that reads `RoomAction.action` via SupabaseTypes (e.g., tooling or future code) would have incorrect type information.
impact: The actual replay path in NetworkClient uses the correct union from `@shared/net/replay`, so there is no runtime error in the hot path. However SupabaseTypes.ts is stale documentation that misleads future maintainers and any code that imports from it to read action rows would get wrong types at compile time. The schema comment in 001_init.sql is similarly stale.
recommendation: Update `RoomAction.action` in SupabaseTypes.ts to `NetworkFireAction | NetworkShieldAction | NetworkBuyAction | NetworkNextRoundAction` (importing or re-declaring the union). Update the schema comment in 001_init.sql to reflect the full action union.

title: restart_game drops the ai field from players — rematch rooms lose CPU seat designation
evidence: restart_game/index.ts lines 128-134: `newPlayers = players.map(p => ({ id: p.id, name: p.name, color: p.color, ready: true, lastSeen: nowMs }))` — the `ai` field from `StoredPlayer` (defined in _shared/mod.ts line 189: `ai?: 'easy' | 'medium' | 'hard'`) is not spread into the new player objects. A room created with CPU seats (bots) that goes to rematch will write a successor room whose `players` JSONB has no `ai` field on any entry. NetworkClient reads `p.ai` to populate `botByTank` (NetworkClient.ts line 184: `if (p.ai) this.botByTank.set(tankId, p.ai)`), so after a rematch all CPU seats become ghost human seats: no bot will drive them, no action will submit for them, and the game freezes on bot turns.
impact: Any room with ≥1 CPU seat that triggers a rematch will produce a broken successor room where bot seats appear human and never act. The game silently freezes. This is a data-integrity defect: a value set at room creation (the ai flag) is silently dropped by a write path (restart_game).
recommendation: In restart_game/index.ts, spread `ai` into the rematch player objects: `newPlayers = players.map(p => ({ id: p.id, name: p.name, color: p.color, ready: true, lastSeen: nowMs, ...(p.ai ? { ai: p.ai } : {}) }))`.
acceptance_criteria: A room with a bot seat that rematches produces a successor room whose players JSONB retains the ai field
NetworkClient.botByTank is populated correctly after rematch
Bot seats drive themselves in the successor game

title: Schema↔code drift: RoomRow in _shared/mod.ts omits status, seed, turn, active_player_index, winner, rematch_room_id — but edge functions select('*') and read those columns
evidence: `RoomRow` in _shared/mod.ts (lines 211-218) declares only `{ id, code, options, players, created_at }`. restart_game/index.ts (line 59) selects `'*'` on the old room and reads `oldRoom.status`, `oldRoom.options`, `oldRoom.players`, `oldRoom.seed`, `oldRoom.active_player_index` (via insert on line 163), and `oldRoom.rematch_room_id` — none of which appear in `RoomRow`. The runtime cast (`as StoredOptions`) works because JS is duck-typed, but the declared type is a lie: TypeScript thinks those fields don't exist, suppressing any compiler check. submit_action/index.ts line 107 similarly does `select('*')` and reads `room.active_player_index`, `room.turn`, `room.players` — none in `RoomRow`.
impact: No runtime error today (ServiceClient is typed `any`, bypassing checks). But any future refactor that narrows the client type will silently break at runtime because the declared shape omits half the columns. Misleading types for maintainers.
recommendation: Extend `RoomRow` in _shared/mod.ts to include all columns accessed by edge functions: `status`, `seed`, `active_player_index`, `turn`, `winner`, `rematch_room_id`. Mark optional where appropriate.
acceptance_criteria: RoomRow declares all columns read by edge functions
Accessing an undeclared column on a RoomRow produces a TS error

title: POSITIVE CONTROL: submit_room_action atomicity and serialization are correctly implemented
evidence: The function: (1) acquires a FOR UPDATE lock on the rooms row before computing MAX(seq)+1, ensuring no two concurrent callers see the same maximum; (2) runs the seq allocation, INSERT, and conditional cursor UPDATE inside a single plpgsql function body, which PostgREST wraps in one transaction — all three mutations commit or roll back together; (3) raises an explicit exception with ERRCODE 'no_data_found' if the room has been deleted between the Edge Function's room-fetch and the plpgsql call (TOCTOU window); (4) REVOKE PUBLIC + GRANT service_role is correctly scoped to the exact arg-type signature. The UNIQUE(room_id,seq) constraint serves as a correct belt-and-suspenders guard. No orphaned action rows or cursor advance without a matching action row is possible through this function.
impact: N/A — this is a positive finding confirming the design is sound.
recommendation: No action required. The VERIFY_004.md recipe should be run as part of any schema reset to confirm the guarantees hold in the deployed environment.

title: POSITIVE CONTROL: Migration ordering and forward-safety are correct across all 5 migrations
evidence: 001 creates rooms + room_actions with all needed constraints and RLS. 002 adds rematch_room_id via ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN (nullable, no lock risk on empty/small table; ON DELETE SET NULL is safe). 003 creates match_scores (new table, no lock on existing tables). 004 uses CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION (no table lock). 005 uses CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS + CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION (idempotent). No migration drops or truncates existing data. No migration depends on data written by a later migration. No circular FK dependencies. RLS posture (public read, service-role write) is consistently applied across all 3 tables.
recommendation: No action required. The only structural note is that 002's ADD COLUMN takes a brief metadata lock; in production Postgres on a large table this is instantaneous (nullable ADD COLUMN is a catalog-only operation in PG 11+, which includes the PG 15 target in config.toml).
